Hi @smutt 
It feels strange to see the TLS class having a different ```__hdr__``` compared to SSL2, essentially packet structure is the same, so why are the fields different?  Secondly, you're creating the ```length``` field as ```len(self.data)```, then you're reducing the size of data but length stays the same. While this approach may work for your use case, it should be following the dpkt style more closely to become part of the library, IMO.  In general looking at ssl.py though, it is a bit of a mess and could use some refactoring.

format_tag = '\?color=tag_index {index} {name}'
format_task = '\?color=task_index {index} {name}'

format_annotation = '\?color=index {index} {description}'
format_tag = '\?color=index {index} {name}'
format_task = '\?color=index {index} {name}'

I can keep it a bit easier... Making them all use same `index` threshold, but then they will have to accept one threshold for all. I'm just playing around... Seeing if we can push py3status beyond its limit... Lol.

Yeah further user-side logging of transport failures for one.

If you start a component with multiple transports this will allow user-code to "do something" with the exception raised as each failed connect attempt happens.  Without this, if one of your transports is unconnectable but another succeeds and you get a working session, your component.start() error callback won't be called so you'll never know other than by looking in logs one of your transports didn't work.

Also could be handy for one-shot connect attempts with one transport and max_retries =  0.  'connectfailure' handler will fire and you'll know quickly there's a fundamental connection error without having to look at the exception type in the component.start() error callback which could be a session level exception.

I'm actually switching the code base over to using this style of asserts.

Historically, Python testing has avoided using the `assert` statement in favor of `assertEqual`, `assertNotNone`, `assertSameContents` and so on.

The reason for this is that in normal Python operation, an assert with a condition that evaluates to False simply throws an exception without any details about what went wrong.

However, when we run tests with py.test (and that is the only supported way to run tests right now), py.test detects these assertion errors and inspects the code to produce a rich error message. This means `assert foo == bar` actually produces more useful output than `assertEqual(foo, bar)` in most cases; for instance, if you assert one dict being equal to another, it will show exactly which keys have differences.
